items.title: People choose to receive human empathy despite rating AI empathy higher
items.author: Michael Inzlicht
items.content: This work explored whether people would rather choose to receive empathy from human or AI empathizers. When given the choice, participants sought human empathy, despite rating AI responses as more empathetic and making them feel more heard.

items.title: Task, person, and experiential characteristics drive the transfer of learning
items.author: Brooke N. Macnamara
items.content: Skill transfer depends on how tasks are structured, who is trained, and how they experience stress. This study demonstrates that individual differences in emotion-cognition traits and physiological responses shape divergent transfer trajectories.

items.title: Perceptual exposure influences body size perceptions and anti-fat attitudes
items.author: K. L. Johnson
items.content: Repeated visual exposure to bodies of varying sizes alters cognitive representations of weight and may influence anti-fat attitudes over time.

items.title: Pre-stimulus alpha power modulates trial-by-trial variability in theta rhythmic multisensory entrainment strength and theta-induced memory effect
items.author: Simon Hanslmayr
items.content: Synchronised rhythmic multisensory entrainment enhances episodic memory performance only when the trials’ actual theta phases follow the stimulation faithfully. Pre-stimulus alpha plays a role in modulating the upcoming trials’ entrainment strength.

items.title: Long-term effects of working memory retrieval from prioritized and deprioritized states
items.author: Bernhard Spitzer
items.content: Testing items in working memory improves long-term memory, especially for deprioritized items. This benefit shows when WM retrieval requires continuous recall, suggesting self-generated reports strengthen subsequent memory.

items.title: Influence of solution efficiency and valence of instruction on additive and subtractive solution strategies in humans, GPT-4, and GPT-4o
items.author: Frank Papenmeier
items.content: Six experiments examined addition bias in humans and in outputs of large language models (GPT-4, GPT-4o). Both human and model-generated solutions showed a preference for additive over subtractive strategies, with a stronger bias in the model output.

items.title: Changes in political attitudes are associated with changes in neural responses to political content
items.author: Yaara Yeshurun
items.content: This study tracked neural responses to political content over two and a half years using fMRI. Changes in political identity influenced neural plasticity, with significant shifts in the amygdala, hippocampus, caudate, and reward systems, reflecting evolving political affiliations.

items.title: Implicit bias attribution reduces prosocial emotions and donation intentions for natural disaster victims
items.author: Jennifer A. Richeson
items.content: How do people respond to biased media reporting attributed to journalists’ implicit versus explicit bias? Attributing bias to implicit rather than explicit attitudes weakens moral emotions, accountability judgments, and support for disaster victims.
